narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream, and we appreciate the courage it takes to put yourself out there amid the challenges many face. Your application included your undergraduate GPA of 2.60, an LSAT score of 31.50, rankings in the 2nd decile for your first-year school grades and the 1st decile for your third-year grades, a middle family income bracket, and plans to attend full-time. The admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on profiles like yours, estimated a 95% chance that you would not pass it. This high predicted risk of failure is why your application was rejected—law schools use this to ensure they admit students likely to succeed on the bar and in practice. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your low ranking in third-year school grades, as it stood out well below what the model sees in typical students who pass. Your middle family income also contributed notably toward the failure prediction, differing from the higher brackets more common among successful bar takers. Your first-year grade ranking played a supporting role in the same direction, again lower than average. Less impactful but still nudging toward failure was your LSAT score, which trailed the typical 36.70 average for those who pass. On a brighter note, your undergraduate GPA slightly favored a passing prediction compared to averages, and your full-time enrollment plan offered a small positive push, aligning with the vast majority of successful students. Overall, your profile ranks lower academically than most who pass the bar, especially in those key grade deciles. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your later school grades through additional coursework or programs, and exploring resources tailored for underrepresented students like scholarships or bar prep support from organizations focused on Black women in law. You're capable of turning this around—many have—and we encourage reapplying after addressing these areas. Keep pushing; your journey matters.
